overlap_description: All four cover constraints on agent *spawning and runtime behavior* (hazards, limitations, failure modes). Config-revert and stalling are both about agent context isolation. Hot-reload and output-files-empty are both about Windows/session-startup sequencing. Low semantic overlap within the 4 — each addresses a distinct failure mode — but they form a natural 'agent gotchas' family.
consolidation_rationale: Each addresses a distinct failure mode with different apply-when triggers. Consolidating would produce a very long compound file. Better approach: group them under a shared section in MEMORY.md ('Agent Operational Gotchas') without merging file content. No files should be deleted.

overlap_description: All four govern *how and how much* to use agents. agent_teams_learnings covers operational patterns (team sizing, explore-is-read-only, token cost). parallel_agents_for_impl covers implementation strategy (use parallel agents + CL). no_incomplete_agent_findings covers completeness requirements. no_agent_budgets covers allocation philosophy. Overlap region: agent_teams_learnings and parallel_agents_for_impl both address when to use parallel vs sequential agent dispatch. The 'subagents vs teams' rule in agent_teams_learnings and the 'parallel agents for implementation' rule in parallel_agents_for_impl cover adjacent ground.
consolidation_rationale: feedback_parallel_agents_for_impl (canonical-only, SoNash canonical) was documented as being absorbed INTO feedback_agent_teams_learnings in the user-home version. This absorption has ALREADY happened in user-home but NOT yet in JASON-OS. JASON-OS has both as separate files, with agent_teams_learnings NOT containing the parallel_agents content. The canonical file feedback_parallel_agents_for_impl.md is an orphan. Recommended: evaluate whether to absorb parallel_agents content into agent_teams_learnings in JASON-OS (matching the SoNash user-home evolution) or keep separate. no_agent_budgets and no_incomplete_agent_findings are sufficiently distinct to keep separate.

overlap_description: MAJOR OVERLAP CLUSTER. All four enforce the same behavioral rule: stop and wait for explicit user input before acting. feedback_ack_requires_approval: never ack/modify state without explicit approval. feedback_interactive_gates: stop at choice prompts, never act on your own question, never bulk-accept ambiguous input. feedback_never_bulk_accept: never interpret ambiguous input as bulk acceptance. feedback_never_defer_without_approval: never defer/skip skill steps without user decision. Critically: feedback_interactive_gates already contains rules 1, 2, and 3 of never_bulk_accept verbatim in its body — the same three rules appear in both files. Rule 2 of interactive_gates ('Never act on a question you just asked') is the same as ack_requires_approval. Rule 3 ('Never interpret ambiguous input as bulk acceptance') is the same as never_bulk_accept.
consolidation_rationale: feedback_interactive_gates is the SUPERSET — it already contains the substance of ack_requires_approval and never_bulk_accept as named sub-rules. These three files (ack_requires_approval, interactive_gates, never_bulk_accept) describe the same behavioral failure mode from different incident angles but with near-identical prescriptions. never_defer_without_approval covers the related but distinct concept of skill-step skipping (not just interactive-gate waiting) — it is a borderline case. Recommended merge: absorb ack_requires_approval and never_bulk_accept INTO interactive_gates. Keep never_defer_without_approval separate (it governs skill execution discipline, not just gate-waiting).

overlap_description: KNOWN SUPERSESSION (pre-documented in Piece 1a §6.2 and D4a findings). feedback_verify_not_grep is the original canonical file (narrower: 'don't use grep for verification'). feedback_grep_vs_understanding is the renamed, broader successor (adds: don't use grep for analysis, replace grep pipelines with structured data). The JASON-OS memory has only feedback_grep_vs_understanding. The SoNash canonical has only feedback_verify_not_grep. The SoNash user-home has only feedback_grep_vs_understanding. This is a rename+expansion, not parallel evolution.
consolidation_rationale: feedback_verify_not_grep in SoNash canonical should be formally superseded by feedback_grep_vs_understanding. The canonical file should be renamed OR a superseded_by field added. JASON-OS correctly has only the new file. No consolidation needed in JASON-OS; the SoNash canonical needs updating. Add supersedes/superseded_by frontmatter to both files.

overlap_description: All five govern PR review workflow but cover distinct aspects: code_review_patterns covers overall discipline (never reject as pre-existing, PR description quality, bot sequencing, pr_timing is embedded as one rule). pr_review_paste_only covers a specific data-sourcing constraint. pr_review_state_files covers state persistence for cross-round/cross-locale work. pr_timing covers a specific timing constraint (don't create PR until ALL work done). no_preexisting_rejection covers a specific rejection policy. Key overlap: feedback_code_review_patterns already contains the 'never reject items as pre-existing' rule AND the 'create PR after session close-out' rule AND 'review bot sequencing' — making no_preexisting_rejection and pr_timing potentially redundant WITH code_review_patterns.
consolidation_rationale: feedback_code_review_patterns is a compound file that has absorbed several rules that also exist as standalone files. Specifically: 'Never reject items as pre-existing' exists in BOTH code_review_patterns AND no_preexisting_rejection. 'Create PR AFTER session close-out' in code_review_patterns maps to pr_timing's core rule. Consider: (a) make code_review_patterns an index/pointer file, expanding standalone files, OR (b) remove the duplicate sub-rules from code_review_patterns and keep it as an overview. pr_review_paste_only and pr_review_state_files are distinct enough to keep separate. no_preexisting_rejection and pr_timing are the consolidation candidates.

overlap_description: HIGH OVERLAP. All three govern the same behavioral failure: jumping ahead of what the user asked. no_premature_next_steps: don't suggest next phase without being asked. no_session_end_assumptions: don't propose session-end as a destination. dont_over_surface: don't enumerate every deferred item when asked 'anything else?'. Key insight: feedback_no_premature_next_steps already contains the substance of no_session_end_assumptions (its last bullet: 'Never propose fold into session-end...') AND the substance of dont_over_surface ('When asked anything else? — name only true blockers. Don't enumerate filed follow-ups...'). The three files are three incident angles for the SAME behavioral failure: over-eagerness to proceed beyond the current task.
consolidation_rationale: feedback_no_premature_next_steps is already a superset of the other two. The session-end rule and the over-surfacing rule both appear as bullet points within it. The standalone no_session_end_assumptions and dont_over_surface files exist as separate memory entries because they were captured in different sessions — but behaviorally they are sub-rules of the same master rule. Recommended: absorb no_session_end_assumptions and dont_over_surface into no_premature_next_steps as named sub-rules. The master file becomes the canonical behavioral rule for 'over-eager progression'.

overlap_description: All four concern how skills are invoked and structured. skills_in_plans_are_tool_calls: slash commands in plans = tool invocations. per_skill_self_audit: every skill needs a self-audit scripted from shared base. no_unnecessary_brainstorming: don't brainstorm when decisions are already made. workflow_chain: brainstorm → deep-research → deep-plan → execute. Key overlap: no_unnecessary_brainstorming and workflow_chain both address when to invoke brainstorming. workflow_chain establishes the sequence; no_unnecessary_brainstorming corrects premature invocation. They are complementary (one says when TO brainstorm, the other says when NOT TO), not redundant.
consolidation_rationale: The four files address different aspects of skill discipline with low content overlap. The brainstorm pair (workflow_chain + no_unnecessary_brainstorming) are complementary not redundant. per_skill_self_audit covers a structural/quality concern. skills_in_plans_are_tool_calls covers an execution concern. No merges needed.

overlap_description: All 9 audit-* single-session skills share: identical frontmatter extensions (supports_parallel, fallback_available, estimated_time_*), same parallel+sequential architecture, same _shared/AUDIT_TEMPLATE.md delegation, same TDMS intake pipeline, same JSONL finding schema (category, title, fingerprint, severity S0-S3, effort E0-E3, confidence, files, why_it_matters, suggested_fix, acceptance_tests). D1e confirmed this: 'single-session audit skills share a family structure.' audit-code, audit-performance, audit-engineering-productivity are described as 'structurally near-identical' by D1f. audit-code and audit-performance share identical frontmatter structure. All use _shared/AUDIT_TEMPLATE.md as a shared protocol document.
consolidation_rationale: These are legitimately different skills with different domain coverage (code vs performance vs security vs documentation). They SHOULD be separate slash commands. The shared structure is intentional — extracted into _shared/AUDIT_TEMPLATE.md. The consolidation opportunity is not merging the skills but: (1) ensuring all 9 use the same frontmatter extension fields uniformly, (2) centralizing the JSONL schema definition rather than repeating it, (3) identifying which domain definitions could be parameterized. For the sync-mechanism, treat as a family bundle that ports together.

overlap_description: The 8 ecosystem audit skills share identical structure. 6 of 8 (tdms, pr, session, doc, skill, health) delegate all shared protocol to _shared/ecosystem-audit/ (5 files: CRITICAL_RULES.md, COMPACTION_GUARD.md, FINDING_WALKTHROUGH.md, SUMMARY_AND_TRENDS.md, CLOSURE_AND_GUARDRAILS.md). hook-ecosystem-audit and script-ecosystem-audit are v2.0 originals that predate the _shared/ extraction and still carry protocol inline. All 8 share the same 6 lib files in scripts/lib/ (safe-fs.js, scoring.js, state-manager.js, parse-jsonl-line.js, patch-generator.js, benchmarks.js). D1a confirmed safe-fs.js is byte-identical across all. D1b confirmed the family pattern: 'script architecture contrast' table shows same layout.
consolidation_rationale: The redundancy has already been addressed by extracting to _shared/ecosystem-audit/. The remaining duplication (inline protocol in hook-ecosystem-audit and script-ecosystem-audit v2.0) is a known tech debt — those two predate the extraction. Future work: migrate hook and script ecosystem audits to use _shared/ delegation like the other 6. lib/ file copies are intentional design (self-contained bundles). For sync-mechanism: treat as a family bundle, port _shared/ecosystem-audit/ first as a prerequisite.

overlap_description: The CAS (Content Analysis System) cluster. analyze, synthesize, and recall form an interdependent pipeline: analyze creates analysis.json + extraction-journal, synthesize reads it to produce SYNTHESIS.md, recall queries the content-analysis.db for prior art. They share: shared/CONVENTIONS.md, scripts/cas/ pipeline scripts, scripts/lib/analysis-schema.js (Zod schema), SQLite backend (.research/content-analysis.db). D1d notes: 'synthesize + analyze + recall triangle forms the CAS — a major SoNash feature cluster.' The conceptual overlap is the shared data contract (analysis.json, last_synthesized_at field), not the functional purpose of each skill.
consolidation_rationale: Each serves a distinct phase: analyze=input, synthesize=transform, recall=query. They cannot be meaningfully merged. The correct treatment for the sync-mechanism is to treat all three as an all-or-nothing port bundle — they share a substrate that must land together. For JASON-OS: none are currently ported. Recommend porting as a bundle once the CAS infrastructure (scripts/cas/, SQLite, analysis-schema.js) is designed for JASON-OS.

overlap_description: BOTH ARE DEPRECATED. website-synthesis and repo-synthesis were both consolidated into /synthesize (T29, Session #271, 2026-04-09). Both are now 2-3KB redirect stubs with identical structure: DEPRECATED notice in H1, superseded_by: synthesize, one-session redirect expiry. D1f explicitly: 'status: deprecated. superseded_by: synthesize.' Both existed before the T29 synthesis consolidation and were replaced when synthesize became the unified synthesis skill.
consolidation_rationale: These are pure redirect stubs scheduled for deletion. They serve only as migration guides for the session immediately after deprecation. In JASON-OS context: neither of these skills exists (synthesize is ported but not in JASON-OS yet either). For the sync-mechanism: do not port either redirect stub. Port only synthesize as the current canonical synthesis skill.

overlap_description: D1f explicitly flags these three as 'structurally near-identical: same YAML frontmatter (supports_parallel, fallback_available, estimated_time_*), same parallel+sequential architecture, same _shared/AUDIT_TEMPLATE.md delegation, same TDMS intake pattern.' Same file layout: SKILL.md only, production status, 9-12KB. D1f notes: 'Any port of one naturally templates the others.' These three plus audit-process and audit-security form the 'single-session audit family'. The frontmatter is described as identical across audit-code, audit-performance, audit-engineering-productivity specifically.
consolidation_rationale: The skills themselves are correctly separate (different domain coverage). The consolidation opportunity is extracting the boilerplate that is currently copy-pasted across all three (and the wider audit family): the parallel mode declaration, the fallback mode description, the TDMS intake steps, the JSONL schema. This is conceptually the same as what _shared/ecosystem-audit/ did for the ecosystem audit family. For JASON-OS sync: when porting any of these three, use one as the template and parameterize the domain-specific content.

overlap_description: All 8 are deprecated redirect stubs consolidated on 2026-04-01. Three patterns: 2→1 (devops-troubleshooter + error-detective → debugger), 1→1 (deployment-engineer → fullstack-developer, react-performance-optimization → performance-engineer), and 3→1 (markdown-syntax-formatter + prompt-engineer + documentation-expert context → technical-writer). D2a and D2b document this comprehensively. All 8 have minimal frontmatter (name + description only), body text pointing to redirect target, expiry 2026-06-01.
consolidation_rationale: These are redirect stubs. The consolidation has already happened. The targets (debugger, fullstack-developer, performance-engineer, technical-writer) are the live agents. Do not port the stubs to JASON-OS. In SoNash, they expire 2026-06-01 — no enforcement mechanism was found.

overlap_description: These 8 agents form the deep-research pipeline. All are in JASON-OS already (per D2a/Piece 1a D2). Each covers a distinct pipeline phase with non-overlapping roles. Structural similarity: all use sonnet model, all have disallowedTools: Agent (prevents recursive spawning). contrarian-challenger and otb-challenger are the most similar pair — both are Phase 3 challenger agents for /deep-research. The key difference: contrarian produces steel-man + pre-mortem; otb produces out-of-the-box alternatives. They are truly complementary, not redundant.
consolidation_rationale: Pipeline agents by design cover different phases. The contrarian/otb pair are distinct roles in the same phase (Phase 3). No consolidation warranted. For sync-mechanism: all 8 are already ported to JASON-OS; the delta between SoNash and JASON-OS versions is only the skills: [sonash-context] frontmatter field.

overlap_description: Both serve context injection: general-purpose (agent) injects SoNash-specific behavioral constraints as a general-purpose responder; sonash-context (skill used as agent context injector) injects stack/architecture context via the skills: [sonash-context] mechanism. The general-purpose agent IS the SoNash context-aware AI assistant for direct user interaction. The sonash-context skill is context injected INTO other agents via frontmatter. They are complementary mechanisms for the same purpose (giving agents SoNash project awareness) but at different layers.
consolidation_rationale: In SoNash, these serve different roles. For JASON-OS: general-purpose should be sanitized and ported (removing SoNash-specific constraints, replacing with JASON-OS constraints). sonash-context should be reimplemented as jason-os-context (a JASON-OS equivalent context injection skill). Neither should be consolidated — they operate at different layers. D1f flagged the jason-os-context port as a high-value mechanism to adopt.

overlap_description: D6a-d found 27+ check-* scripts in scripts/. D6b confirmed a subset are near-identical: check-pattern-compliance.js, check-cross-doc-deps.js, and check-schema-contract.js all have the same structure (read JSONL, validate against schema, report violations). Several check-* scripts were confirmed to share identical error-reporting patterns and exit-code conventions. However, the actual check logic differs per domain. The naming convention groups them, but content analysis is needed to identify true redundancy.
consolidation_rationale: Without reading every check-* script in detail, conservative assessment is: many are domain-specific with shared structure. The consolidation opportunity is a shared check-runner infrastructure (common argument parsing, output formatting, exit code conventions) rather than merging the domain logic. This mirrors what _shared/ecosystem-audit/ did for skill protocol. Recommend a dedicated check-infrastructure.js shared lib before any merges.

overlap_description: D1a confirmed: safe-fs.js is BYTE-IDENTICAL across all 5 mega-skill copies. D1b confirms the same pattern for the remaining ecosystem audits and alerts. The canonical copy lives at scripts/lib/safe-fs.js. The skill-local copies exist under each skill's scripts/lib/ subdirectory. This is a copy-not-symlink pattern. D1a notes: 'Any fix to the canonical must be propagated to all 5 skill copies. This is a maintenance hazard not a portability blocker.'
consolidation_rationale: The copy pattern is intentional (self-contained skill bundles). The consolidation opportunity is not elimination but documentation: the canonical source must be the scripts/lib/ version, and any update must be propagated to all copies. For the sync-mechanism, safe-fs.js should be treated as a single unit that syncs to all locations. JASON-OS already has scripts/lib/safe-fs.js — the skill-local copies should use the same canonical approach.

overlap_description: D1f notes 7 similar but distinct self-audit.js scripts across skill subdirectories. Each runs mechanical verification of that skill's invariants. feedback_per_skill_self_audit (JASON-OS memory) documents the pattern: 'Almost every skill needs its own self-audit script, generated from a shared base version of skill-audit and adjusted for that skill's specific invariants.' These scripts share: JSONL output format, common structural checks (frontmatter presence, line count, MUST/SHOULD hierarchy), common error-reporting patterns. They differ in: skill-specific invariant checks (deep-plan checks decision-record standalone-ness; deep-research checks phase boundaries; checkpoint checks state-file contract).
consolidation_rationale: The shared structural checks should live in a base module (scripts/skills/shared/self-audit-base.js or similar) imported by each skill's self-audit. The skill-specific checks remain in each file. This is explicitly the recommendation in feedback_per_skill_self_audit: 'scripting from a base means the structural checks stay uniform and only the skill-specific extensions diverge.' This is the correct consolidation pattern. No files should be deleted; instead, refactor to import from shared base.
